q: Which of the following Acts established the Supreme Court at Calcutta?
explanation: The Regulating Act of 1773 provided for the establishment of a Supreme Court at Calcutta (1774) comprising one chief justice and three other judges.

q: Who was designated as the first Governor-General of India?
explanation: The Charter Act of 1833 made the Governor-General of Bengal the 'Governor-General of India'. Lord William Bentinck was the first to hold this title.

q: The system of 'Communal Electorate' in India was first introduced by which Act?
explanation: The Indian Councils Act of 1909 (Minto-Morley Reforms) introduced a system of communal representation for Muslims.

q: Which Act abolished 'Dyarchy' in the provinces and introduced 'Provincial Autonomy'?
explanation: The GoI Act 1935 abolished the dyarchy introduced in 1919 in the provinces and replaced it with 'provincial autonomy'.

q: The Fundamental Rights in the Indian Constitution are inspired by the Constitution of which country?
explanation: Part III of the Constitution (Fundamental Rights) draws its inspiration from the Bill of Rights of the USA.

q: Which of the following Fundamental Rights is available ONLY to citizens of India and NOT to foreigners?
explanation: Articles 15, 16, 19, 29, and 30 are available exclusively to Indian citizens. Articles 14, 21, and 25 are available to both.

q: Article 17 of the Indian Constitution deals with the abolition of:
explanation: Article 17 abolishes 'untouchability' and forbids its practice in any form. It is an absolute right.

q: By which Constitutional Amendment was the Right to Property deleted from the list of Fundamental Rights?
explanation: The 44th Amendment Act (1978) deleted Article 31 and made the Right to Property a legal right under Article 300A in Part XII.

q: Which Article of the Constitution guarantees the right to free and compulsory education for children aged 6 to 14?
explanation: Article 21A, inserted by the 86th Amendment Act in 2002, makes elementary education a Fundamental Right.

q: Which two Articles CANNOT be suspended even during a National Emergency?
explanation: The 44th Amendment Act (1978) mandated that the President cannot suspend the right to move the Court for the enforcement of fundamental rights guaranteed by Articles 20 and 21.

q: Which writ translates to 'We Command'?
explanation: Mandamus literally means 'we command'. It is a command issued by the court to a public official asking him to perform his official duties.

q: The Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms (1919) are famous for introducing which of the following features?
explanation: The GoI Act 1919 (Mont-Ford Reforms) introduced a dual scheme of governance in the provinces known as 'dyarchy' (divided into transferred and reserved subjects).

q: Which Act created the office of the 'Secretary of State for India'?
explanation: The GoI Act 1858 abolished the Board of Control and Court of Directors and created a new office, Secretary of State for India, vested with complete authority.

q: Article 24 of the Indian Constitution strictly prohibits:
options: Employment of children below 14 years in factories
explanation: Article 24 prohibits the employment of children below the age of 14 years in any factory, mine, or other hazardous activities.

q: What does the Latin writ term 'Quo-Warranto' mean?
explanation: Quo-Warranto is issued by the court to inquire into the legality of a claim of a person to a public office, preventing illegal usurpation.

q: Which of the following rights was considered by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar as the 'Heart and Soul' of the Constitution?
options: Right to Life and Personal Liberty
explanation: Dr. Ambedkar called Article 32 (Right to Constitutional Remedies) the heart and soul because it makes all other fundamental rights enforceable.

q: The doctrine of 'Judicial Review' in India is explicitly based on which Article of the Constitution?
explanation: Article 13 declares that all laws that are inconsistent with or in derogation of any of the fundamental rights shall be void, which is the basis of Judicial Review.

q: Which Act separated, for the first time, the legislative and executive functions of the Governor-General’s council?
explanation: The Charter Act of 1853 established a separate Governor-General’s legislative council which came to be known as the Indian (Central) Legislative Council.

q: Protection against 'Double Jeopardy' is guaranteed under which Article?
explanation: Article 20(2) states that no person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offense more than once (double jeopardy).

q: The establishment of a Federal Court in India was recommended by:
explanation: The GoI Act 1935 provided for the establishment of a Federal Court, which was set up in 1937.
